agent_output:
# Domain Analysis — Driver Earnings Transparency Feature

## Domain Analysis

### Business Context

This feature encodes the **compensation visibility** business process within the **Driver Compensation** bounded context. It intersects critically with **Labor Compliance**, **Payroll**, and **Regional Operations** contexts. The core challenge is not the UI — it's that "earnings," "driver," and even "deduction" mean fundamentally different things depending on jurisdiction and employment classification. A single global implementation that ignores these distinctions will be **legally non-compliant on day one**.

### Ubiquitous Language

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Earnings Breakdown** | A jurisdiction-aware, itemized view of a driver's compensation for a given period. NOT a single global schema — the line items, labels, and legal disclosures vary by region. |
| **Base Pay** | In employee contexts (CA): the guaranteed hourly/daily wage floor. In contractor contexts (ID): the per-task or per-delivery base rate. These are legally distinct concepts sharing a colloquial name. |
| **Per-Delivery Bonus** | Variable compensation tied to delivery completion. In CA, must be clearly distinguished from base pay to avoid wage-and-hour violations. In ID, may be the primary compensation mechanism. |
| **Tip** | Gratuity from the end customer passed through to the driver. In CA under AB5, tips MUST NOT count toward minimum wage obligations. In ID, platform may take a cut depending on contract terms. |
| **Deduction** | Any amount subtracted from gross earnings. In CA, deductions must be individually authorized and comply with Labor Code §221. In Germany, mandatory deductions (taxes, social insurance) must be itemized per Entgeltnachweis standards. In ID, deductions are governed by the contractor agreement. |
| **Driver (Employee)** | A worker classified under local employment law (CA under AB5, Germany under standard labor law). Entitled to wage protections, itemized pay statements, and minimum wage guarantees. |
| **Driver (Independent Contractor)** | A worker in a contractor relationship (Indonesia). Compensation is contractual, not wage-regulated. The earnings display must NOT use language that implies employment. |
| **Works Council (Betriebsrat)** | German co-determination body with legal authority over changes to monitoring and compensation display systems under BetrVG §87. Their approval is a **hard prerequisite**, not a courtesy. |
| **Itemized Wage Statement** | A legally mandated document (CA Labor Code §226). The earnings breakdown in CA is not merely a "nice to have" — it IS this legal artifact and must meet statutory requirements. |
| **Real-Time** | In this context: updated after each completed delivery or pay-relevant event within the current pay period. Does NOT mean sub-second streaming — means "current as of last completed transaction." |
| **Pay Period** | The time boundary for earnings aggregation. Weekly, biweekly, or monthly depending on jurisdiction and contract terms. Earnings breakdown must be scoped to a pay period, not shown as an unbounded running total. |

### Bounded Context

**Driver Compensation** — with mandatory integration points to:
- **Regional Compliance** (jurisdiction-specific rules engine)
- **Payroll** (authoritative source of earnings data)
- **Driver Identity** (employment classification, jurisdiction assignment)

---

## Business Rules

| # | Rule | Invariant? | Testable Assertion |
|---|---|---|---|
| **BR-1** | The earnings breakdown schema MUST vary by jurisdiction and employment classification. A single flat schema is a domain violation. | ✅ Yes | Given a CA-employee driver and an ID-contractor driver, their earnings breakdowns must contain different line item sets and legal labels. |
| **BR-2** | In California, tips MUST be displayed as a separate line item and MUST NOT be included in or offset against base pay calculations. | ✅ Yes | Given a CA driver with $50 base pay and $20 tips, the breakdown shows base pay = $50 and tips = $20 separately. Base pay is never reduced by tip amount. |
| **BR-3** | In California, the earnings breakdown MUST satisfy Labor Code §226 requirements for itemized wage statements: gross wages, total hours, piece-rate units (deliveries), all deductions, net wages, pay period dates, and employer identity. | ✅ Yes | Given a CA driver viewing their earnings, ALL §226 required fields are present. Omission of any field = non-compliant. |
| **BR-4** | In Germany, the feature MUST NOT be deployed to drivers until Betriebsrat approval is formally obtained and recorded. | ✅ Yes | Given Betriebsrat approval status = "pending" for DE region, the feature flag for DE drivers returns disabled. No bypass exists. |
| **BR-5** | In Germany, the breakdown must conform to Entgeltnachweis (pay slip) standards, including mandatory social insurance contributions, tax withholdings, and church tax where applicable. | ✅ Yes | Given a DE driver viewing earnings, the deductions section itemizes income tax (Lohnsteuer), solidarity surcharge, health insurance, pension insurance, unemployment insurance, and care insurance as separate line items. |
| **BR-6** | In Indonesia, the earnings display MUST NOT use employment-implying language (e.g., "wages," "salary," "employee"). Doing so risks reclassification liability. | ✅ Yes | Given an ID driver, no label in the earnings breakdown contains the words "wage," "salary," "employee," or local-language equivalents. Terms like "earnings," "payout," "service fee" are used instead. |
| **BR-7** | Deductions in California require individual written authorization from the driver (Labor Code §221). The system must not display unauthorized deductions. | ✅ Yes | Given a CA driver, every deduction line item maps to a signed authorization record. Any deduction without authorization must be flagged/blocked. |
| **BR-8** | Earnings data displayed must be sourced from the Payroll system of record. The transparency feature is a **read-only projection** — it must never be the source of truth for compensation. | ✅ Yes | Given any driver viewing earnings, all displayed values trace to Payroll ledger entries. No earnings data is calculated or stored independently by the transparency feature. |
| **BR-9** | "Real-time" means updated within one pay-relevant event cycle (delivery completion, adjustment posted). Stale data beyond this window must show a "last updated" timestamp and must never show partial/in-flight transactions. | ✅ Yes | Given a driver who completed a delivery 5 minutes ago, the earnings breakdown reflects that delivery. In-progress (unconfirmed) deliveries are never shown. |
| **BR-10** | Each jurisdiction's earnings breakdown must be displayed in the local language AND local currency. | ✅ Yes | Given a DE driver, the breakdown is in German using EUR. Given an ID driver, it's in Bahasa Indonesia using IDR. |

---

## Business Impact

### Financial
- **California**: Non-compliance with §226 exposes the platform to **$50–$4,000 per pay period per driver** in statutory penalties. With thousands of drivers, this is an existential-scale liability.
- **Germany**: Deploying without Betriebsrat approval can trigger injunctions, fines, and invalidation of the entire feature — sunk development cost.
- **Indonesia**: Misclassification risk from employment-implying language could trigger retroactive employment benefits liability (severance, social security, leave).
- **Upside**: Earnings transparency is strongly correlated with driver retention. A 5% improvement in retention can save millions in recruitment and onboarding costs annually.

### Operational
- Requires a **jurisdiction-aware rules engine** — not just i18n, but different domain logic per region. This is a permanent architectural commitment.
- Payroll integration must be near-real-time and highly reliable. Any lag or inconsistency between Payroll and the display erodes driver trust instantly.
- Germany rollout has a **non-deterministic timeline** — Betriebsrat negotiations can take weeks to months. The feature must be deployable per-region independently.

### User (Driver) Impact
- **High positive impact** when done correctly. Pay transparency is the #1 driver-requested feature across gig platforms globally.
- **High negative impact** if done incorrectly. Showing wrong numbers, missing deductions, or confusing labels will generate support tickets at scale and erode trust faster than no feature at all.
- Drivers will screenshot and share breakdowns. Every label, number, and calculation must withstand public scrutiny.

### Risk Profile
- **Legal risk (HIGH)**: Three jurisdictions, three fundamentally different compliance regimes. A "build once, deploy everywhere" approach is the highest-risk architectural choice.
- **Reclassification risk (MEDIUM)**: Indonesia terminology must be carefully vetted. One "wage" label in a contractor context can be used as evidence in reclassification lawsuits.
- **Data accuracy risk (HIGH)**: Showing drivers incorrect earnings is worse than showing nothing. Payroll must be the single source of truth with no independent calculations.

### Stakeholders Affected

| Stakeholder Group | Impact |
|---|---|
| **Drivers (CA)** | Legally entitled to this information. Positive impact on trust and retention. Will compare against pay stubs — numbers must match exactly. |
| **Drivers (DE)** | Cannot receive the feature until Betriebsrat approves. Must be clearly communicated to avoid frustration during the gap. |
| **Drivers (ID)** | Positive impact on trust, but language must be carefully contractor-framed to avoid legal exposure for the platform. |
| **Legal/Compliance** | Must sign off on per-jurisdiction label sets, disclosures, and deduction authorization flows before launch. |
| **Betriebsrat (DE)** | Has co-determination rights. Must be engaged as a partner, not informed as a stakeholder. Blocking power is real. |
| **Finance/Payroll** | Must provide near-real-time data feed. Any payroll system changes affect this feature directly. |
| **Customer Support** | Will receive inquiries about earnings discrepancies. Needs training and escalation paths before launch. |
| **Product/Growth** | Earnings transparency is a competitive differentiator for driver acquisition. Delay in any market is a competitive risk. |

---

## Acceptance Criteria

### Core Criteria

| # | Given | When | Then |
|---|---|---|---|
| **AC-1** | A California employee-driver has completed 3 deliveries in the current pay period | They open the earnings breakdown | They see: gross wages, base pay (hourly), per-delivery bonus × 3, tips (separate line), itemized deductions (each authorized), net pay, total hours, pay period dates, employer name and address |
| **AC-2** | A Germany employee-driver exists AND Betriebsrat approval = NOT granted | The feature is deployed globally | The earnings breakdown is NOT visible to any DE driver. No workaround, no "preview mode," no beta. |
| **AC-3** | A Germany employee-driver exists AND Betriebsrat approval = granted | They open the earnings breakdown | They see: gross pay, itemized statutory deductions (Lohnsteuer, Solidaritätszuschlag, health/pension/unemployment/care insurance), net pay, all in German, all in EUR |
| **AC-4** | An Indonesia contractor-driver views their earnings | They inspect every label and term in the UI | Zero instances of "wage," "salary," "employee," or Bahasa equivalents ("gaji," "upah," "karyawan"). Only contractor-safe terms appear. |
| **AC-5** | A CA driver has a deduction on their breakdown | They check the deduction against authorization records | Every displayed deduction has a corresponding signed authorization. Unauthorized deductions are never displayed — they are routed to compliance review. |
| **AC-6** | A driver completes a delivery | They check their earnings within 5 minutes | The completed delivery's earnings contribution is reflected. No in-progress or unconfirmed deliveries appear. |
| **AC-7** | The Payroll system has a specific value for a driver's earnings | The transparency feature displays that driver's earnings | The values match exactly. The transparency feature performs no independent calculation — it is a read-only projection. |

### Edge Cases

| Scenario | Expected Behavior |
|---|---|
| Driver relocates from Indonesia to California mid-pay-period | Earnings breakdown splits at the jurisdiction transition point. CA portion follows CA rules; ID portion follows ID rules. No blending. |
| Tip is adjusted (increased/decreased) after initial display | Earnings breakdown updates to reflect the adjustment with an audit trail visible to the driver. Previous amounts are not silently overwritten. |
| Betriebsrat approval is revoked after initial DE rollout | Feature is immediately disabled for all DE drivers. Cached/offline views are invalidated. |
| Payroll system is temporarily unavailable | Earnings breakdown shows "last updated" timestamp and a clear "data may not reflect recent activity" notice. Stale data is NEVER presented as current. |
| Driver has zero earnings for current pay period | Breakdown displays $0/€0/Rp0 with all expected line items at zero. Does not show "no data" or hide the feature — the empty state IS a valid earnings statement. |
| A CA driver's base pay + bonuses fall below minimum wage for hours worked | The system must surface the minimum wage guarantee top-up as a visible line item. It cannot be hidden inside base pay. |
| Platform takes a service fee from an ID contractor's earnings | Displayed as a clearly labeled platform fee, not as a "deduction" (which implies employment). The gross-to-net flow must be transparent. |

---

## Confidence Assessment

```
confidence:
  level: 85
  high_confidence_areas:
    - "California AB5 and §226 requirements are well-established law"
    - "Betriebsrat co-determination rights under BetrVG §87 are unambiguous"
    - "Tip separation rules in CA are settled employment law"
    - "Read-only projection pattern for earnings display"
    - "Contractor vs employee language risk in Indonesia"
  low_confidence_areas:
    - "Exact Entgeltnachweis format requirements for digital display (vs paper pay slip) — may need German labor counsel review"
    - "Bahasa Indonesia specific prohibited terms — need local legal review for completeness"
    - "Whether Indonesia has emerging pay transparency regulations in pipeline"
    - "Precise SLA for 'real-time' — platform-specific latency tolerances not defined"
    - "Whether DE works council scope extends to contractor-drivers if any exist in DE"
  assumptions:
    - "All CA drivers are classified as employees under AB5 (no carve-out exceptions)"
    - "Germany operations have an established Betriebsrat (companies above threshold)"
    - "Indonesia drivers are genuinely independent contractors under current local law"
    - "Payroll system can support near-real-time data feeds"
    - "Feature is mobile-first (driver app), not back-office tooling"
```

---

## Rejection Reasons (Preemptive)

Any implementation that exhibits the following will be **rejected**:

| Violation | Business Rule Broken |
|---|---|
| Single global earnings schema with no jurisdiction variance | BR-1: Breakdown must vary by jurisdiction and classification |
| Tips folded into "total compensation" in California | BR-2: Tips must be separate, must not offset base pay |
| German rollout without documented Betriebsrat approval gate | BR-4: Hard prerequisite, not optional |
| Using "wages" or "salary" anywhere in the Indonesian driver experience | BR-6: Reclassification liability |
| Earnings calculated independently from Payroll | BR-8: Transparency feature is a read-only projection, never a source of truth |
| Feature deployed as a single global toggle (all regions or none) | BR-4 + operational: Must be independently deployable per jurisdiction |
| Deductions shown without authorization verification in CA | BR-7: Every CA deduction requires signed authorization |

---

**Bottom line:** This is not a UI feature — it is a **multi-jurisdiction labor compliance artifact** that happens to have a user interface. The architecture must encode jurisdiction as a first-class domain concept, not a configuration flag. Build it wrong and you're not just showing bad numbers — you're generating legal evidence against yourself.
